cataloging_subject.label: The Wright's Chaste Wife
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The late Middle English comic verse tale conventionally called The Wright's Chaste Wife. The record describes the intellectual work represented by CME00054, not Lambeth Palace Library MS 306 as a whole, Furnivall's revised second edition, the University of Michigan encoding, the repository XML, or the medical recipes and other works adjacent to the poem in the codex.

catalog_summary.title: The Wright's Chaste Wife
catalog_summary.author: Adam of Cobsam (attributed; authorship uncertain)
catalog_summary.date: Traditionally dated c. 1462; uniquely attested in a manuscript copy dated to the 1460s
catalog_summary.region: England; precise place of composition unknown

titles.value: A ffable of a Wryght that was maryde to a pore wydows dowter the whiche wydow havyng noo good to geve with her gave as for a precyous Iohell to hym a Rose garlond the whyche sche affermyd wold never fade while sche kept truly her wedlok

scope.description: Title added by a later hand at Lambeth MS 306, fol. 178r

notes: This is a later manuscript title, not evidence for the wording of an authorial title.

agents.name: Adam of Cobsam
notes: Named in the poem at line 620; no independently verified biography is known from the sources consulted.

assertion.notes: The line 'Thys seyd Adam of Cobsam' supports traditional attribution but does not unambiguously distinguish author, teller, or source authority; METS categorizes the authorship as uncertain.

scope.description: The 1865 first edition and the immediate 1869 revised second edition
Direct transcription of Lambeth MS 306, fols. 178r-187r

notes: Furnivall states that he copied the poem from the manuscript in one afternoon after William Stubbs showed him the volume.

date_statements.display: Traditionally c. 1462, inferred from the poem's concluding White Rose and Edward IV allusion; exact composition date uncertain
notes: Furnivall's title page prints 'about 1462 A.D.' and his preface dates the handwriting soon after 1460. Neither statement securely proves the work's composition year.

date_statements.display: 1460s, the date assigned by the modern METS bibliography to the unique Lambeth MS 306 witness
notes: This dates the surviving copy and first known attestation, not necessarily composition. The Lambeth catalog conservatively dates Scribe H to the fifteenth century.

date_statements.display: First published by Frederick J. Furnivall in EETS Original Series 12 (1865)

notes: The poem is Middle English and its conclusion invokes England; this does not establish a precise place of composition. London associations in the codex and modern criticism are not promoted to a composition claim.

form_statements.description: A 672-line narrative poem in six-line aabccb stanzas; editorial prose summaries and notes in Furnivall and CME are not part of the work's form.

genre_statements.label: Social satire
notes: METS places the poem in 'Satire and Fabliaux in Verse and Prose'; its notes cite DuVal's description of it as a satiric fabliau.

subject_statements.label: Domestic labor, craft, and gender roles
notes: The wife forces the lord, steward, and proctor to process flax and spin for food; METS emphasizes female domestic labor in its contextual framing.

subject_statements.label: Social rank, labor, and comic humiliation
White Rose and Yorkist political allusion
notes: The political interpretation is Furnivall's; the poem itself names white roses, England, God, and the commons without explicitly naming Edward IV.

summaries.value: A wright marries a reputedly faithful woman and receives a rose garland whose color is said to reveal her fidelity. While he is away building for his lord, the lord, steward, and proctor each offer his wife money for sex. She accepts their gifts, traps each would-be lover in a deep chamber, and makes the three men process flax and spin in exchange for food. When the wright returns, the men are exposed and released, the wife keeps their money, and the unfaded garland confirms her fidelity.

CME00054 contains Furnivall's edition title page and the complete 672-line poem with marginal summaries and six footnotes. It does not encode Furnivall's preface, printed notes, glossary, the companion poem 'Women,' or the surrounding contents of Lambeth MS 306.

extent.description: One poem. The repository XML contains 672 L elements in six large LG containers; DIMEV independently describes 672 lines in six-line aabccb stanzas. The six XML LG containers are encoding groupings, not a claim that the poem has only six stanzas.

related_works.title: Gesta Romanorum, chapter 69, De castitate
notes: Furnivall's 1869 postscript calls Keller's chapter 69 the poem's immediate original. Modern METS contextualizes Gesta material as analogue and related tradition. This relation is retained as an attributed, unresolved source hypothesis rather than a certain direct-transmission claim.

evidence.citation: General Corpus pinned CME source XML, CME00054.xml.
evidence.locator: title-page DIV1; poem DIV1; lines 1-672; final review TEMPHEAD
evidence.summary: Establishes the exact encoded title page, preferred poem title, Adam attribution line, 672-line text, language, line and leaf milestones, marginal summaries, six footnotes, italics, and selection boundary.

evidence.citation: Element and attribute audit of repository CME00054.xml.
evidence.locator: ETS/EEBO/TEXT with direct counts of L, LG, NOTE, PB, MILESTONE, HI, and ABBR
evidence.summary: Counts 672 L, 6 LG, 202 NOTE (196 marginal and 6 foot), 20 PB, 242 MILESTONE, 110 italic HI spans, and 12 ABBR elements. These are encoding metrics, not universal work structure.

evidence.citation: Frederick J. Furnivall, ed., The Wright's Chaste Wife, revised second edition (EETS Original Series 12, 1869, retaining the 1865 imprint), complete Harvard/Google scan at Internet Archive.
evidence.locator: title page at PDF p. 8; preface at PDF pp. 10-13; poem at PDF pp. 14-32 / printed pp. 1-19
evidence.summary: Primary evidence for the 1869 revised-edition statement, 1865 retained imprint, editor/transcriber role, one-afternoon manuscript transcription, traditional dating, the claimed Gesta source, and the exact CME selection within the volume.

evidence.citation: Lambeth Palace Library Archives Catalogue, MS 306, item 26.
evidence.locator: item 26, ff. 178r-187r; physical description; Booklet 10; Copies
evidence.summary: Confirms the exact work locus, Scribe H, unique booklet context, fifteenth-century hand, full codex extent and binding, adjacent medical recipes, and the public digital-copy and microfilm routes.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, DIMEV/NIMEV 252, record-432; witness wit-432-1; Lam306; Furnivall1865a.
evidence.locator: data/Records.xml record-432 and wit-432-1; data/Manuscripts.xml Lam306; data/Bibliography.xml Furnivall1865a
evidence.summary: Current scholarly index evidence for title, first line, 672-line six-line aabccb stanza form, chastity and tale subjects, unique Lambeth witness, manuscript title and attribution, and Furnivall edition history.

evidence.citation: Eve Salisbury, ed., The Wright's Chaste Wife, in The Trials and Joys of Marriage, Middle English Texts Series (2002).
evidence.locator: work text PDF pp. 62-79; select bibliography and notes PDF pp. 80-85
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly comparison edition supporting fabliau and satire classification, 1460s witness dating, uncertain authorship context, manuscript locus, bibliography, synopsis, gender, labor, and marriage themes.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for CME00054.
evidence.summary: Binds the qualified Gesta source claim, exact edition sequence, complete-codex facsimile, access, and rights evidence to the descriptive record.

open_questions.question: Does the line naming Adam of Cobsam identify the author, a performer or teller, or an earlier source authority?
next_steps: Consult specialist authorship studies and any paleographical analysis of the name in Lambeth MS 306.
Retain attributed authorship unless direct evidence distinguishes the role.

open_questions.question: Can composition be dated or localized more securely than the traditional c. 1462/1460s inference from the unique witness and White Rose allusion?
next_steps: Review current scholarship on the political allusion, language, and Scribe H's booklet context.
Do not infer London composition from the miscellany's London materials or later ownership.

open_questions.question: Is Keller's Gesta Romanorum chapter 69 a direct textual source for The Wright's Chaste Wife or one analogue within a broader tale tradition?
next_steps: Compare the English poem with multiple Gesta chapter-69 witnesses and other chastity-test and trapped-suitor analogues.
Seek modern source scholarship before strengthening the relation beyond Furnivall's 1869 assertion.

notes: The manuscript's exact work locus is ff. 178r-187r within Booklet 10; the complete public facsimile is mapped in the linked lineage manifest rather than reduced to a work-only excerpt.
Furnivall's printed italic letters frequently represent manuscript abbreviation expansions or supplied letters, not modern semantic emphasis.

The U-M browse page currently labels the Furnivall item '(1875)'; the inspected title page and DIMEV establish first edition 1865 and revised second edition 1869, so 1875 is retained only as a catalog-layer error in lineage evidence.
